title: The July Occupancy Drop That Every Childcare Centre Owner Needs to Plan For
linkedin:
Every July, childcare centre occupancy drops. The cost base does not.

That gap between a near-full June and a quieter July is one of the most predictable financial events in the childcare calendar. What is less predictable is how many centre owners arrive at July without a clear plan for managing it.

The reason the drop bites so hard is the NQF. Minimum educator-to-child ratios do not move with session count. Whether a room is at capacity or at sixty percent occupancy during school holidays, the minimum educator complement stays. The wage cost of that floor does not flex with bookings.

So the same cost base that supports a full June room is still there in a quieter July room. Occupancy percentage drives room-level profitability directly. In some rooms, the school holiday period is the difference between margin and loss.

There is also a CCS dimension. After EOFY, Services Australia reconciles actual family income against the estimates used to calculate Child Care Subsidy payments. Families who earned more than estimated may owe subsidy back. For some, that notice lands in July, prompting a session review and secondary occupancy softness through August.

The most effective planning for July happens in June. Three things make a consistent difference: a 13-week cash flow forecast through September, a room-level contribution margin review at holiday-period occupancy, and any fee increase communicated in June for August implementation.

If your June occupancy is solid and your July position has not been mapped, there are about three weeks left to change that.

Full piece on the ProfitPulse blog.

July is the sharpest occupancy dip in the childcare calendar, and the reason it catches centres short is simple: educator ratios don't flex when bookings do.

Whether a room is running at full capacity or sixty percent during school holidays, the minimum educator complement stays. Same wages, fewer sessions.

If you run a childcare or early education centre, how does your July cash position look right now? Have you mapped it against your committed outflows?

New post on the ProfitPulse blog covers the NQF cost structure, the CCS reconciliation timing, and what to do about it before June 30. https://profit-pulse.com.au/the-july-occupancy-drop-that-every-childcare-centre-owner-needs-to-plan-for

google:
Childcare and early childhood education operators in Queensland, NSW, and Victoria: the July school holidays create the sharpest occupancy drop of the year. Most centre owners know this from experience. Not as many have built the financial plan that reflects it before it arrives.

The reason July concentrates financial risk is structural. The National Quality Framework sets minimum educator-to-child ratios that do not move with bookings. Whether a room is full or at sixty percent capacity during the holiday period, the minimum educator complement stays in place. The same wage cost supports a smaller revenue pool.

There is also the Child Care Subsidy reconciliation. After June 30, Services Australia compares actual family income against the estimates used to calculate CCS payments throughout the year. For some families, that reconciliation arrives in July, prompting a review of household spending that can include reducing care sessions.

The most effective response to July is planned in June. A 13-week cash flow forecast extended through September maps the occupancy drop against committed outflows. A room-level cost review using EOFY data shows which rooms are genuinely contribution-positive and which cross-subsidise others during holiday periods. If a fee increase is planned for the new year, communicating it in June for August implementation separates the pricing conversation from the school holiday period.

ProfitPulse has published a post this week that walks through all three of these steps for childcare and early education operators.

Read it here: https://profit-pulse.com.au/the-july-occupancy-drop-that-every-childcare-centre-owner-needs-to-plan-for
